fact: January 11 is exactly 11 days into the new year, which means you've already survived 1.6% of the entire year!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many squirrels start getting super cranky because their buried nuts are frozen solid underground.
The sun rises about 1 minute earlier each day around January 11 in most places, even though it's still freezing cold outside.
January 11 falls smack in the middle of Capricorn season, when people born under this zodiac sign are supposedly extra stubborn like mountain goats.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, January 11 is peak summer - perfect for finding cicadas that sound like tiny chainsaws in the trees.
This date sits exactly 3 weeks and 1 day after the winter solstice, meaning daylight is slowly creeping back in the north.
Many evergreen trees are still dropping their oldest needles around this time, creating crunchy brown carpets that smell like Christmas.
January 11 is when polar bears in the Arctic are at their fluffiest - their winter coats are so thick they look like walking marshmallows.
The moon phase on January 11 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it appears extra bright because of all the snow reflecting moonlight.
In cold regions, January 11 is prime time for finding animal tracks in fresh snow - like a natural detective game frozen in your backyard.
This is when many houseplants go into their deepest winter sleep mode and basically ignore you for weeks, no matter how much you water them.
January 11 marks roughly the time when winter sunsets start shifting from orange to deep purple because of how low the sun hangs in the sky.
